From Wikipedia, the free encyclopedia

British weekly satirical magazine, 1841–2002

This article is about the British magazine. For other publications, see [Punch § Periodicals](/wiki/Punch_(disambiguation)#Periodicals "Punch (disambiguation)").

Not to be confused with [Puck (magazine)](/wiki/Puck_(magazine) "Puck (magazine)").

*Punch, or The London Charivari*

|  |  |
| --- | --- |
| Cover of the first *Punch, or The London Charivari*, depicts Punch hanging a caricatured [Devil](/wiki/Devil "Devil"), 1841 (see [gallery below](#Gallery_of_selected_early_covers) for enlarged detail). | |

| Frequency | Weekly |

***Punch, or The London Charivari*** was a British weekly magazine of [humour](/wiki/Humour "Humour") and [satire](/wiki/Satire "Satire") established in 1841 by [Henry Mayhew](/wiki/Henry_Mayhew "Henry Mayhew") and wood-engraver [Ebenezer Landells](/wiki/Ebenezer_Landells "Ebenezer Landells"). Historically, it was most influential in the 1840s and 1850s, when it helped to coin the term "[cartoon](/wiki/Cartoon "Cartoon")" in its modern sense as a humorous illustration. Artists at *Punch* included [John Tenniel](/wiki/John_Tenniel "John Tenniel") who worked for the magazine from 1850 and was its chief cartoonist from 1864 to 1901. The editors took the anarchic puppet Mr Punch, of [Punch and Judy](/wiki/Punch_and_Judy "Punch and Judy"), as their mascot—the character appears in many magazine covers—with the character also an inspiration for the magazine's name.

With its satire of the contemporary, social, and political scene, *Punch* became a household name in [Victorian Britain](/wiki/Victorian_Britain "Victorian Britain"). Sales of 40,000 copies a week by 1850 rose above 100,000 by 1910. After the 1940s, when its circulation peaked, it went into a long decline, closing in 1992. It was revived in 1996, but closed again in 2002.

## History

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Punch_(magazine)&action=edit&section=1 "Edit section: History")]

*Punch* was founded on 17 July 1841 by [Henry Mayhew](/wiki/Henry_Mayhew "Henry Mayhew") and wood-engraver [Ebenezer Landells](/wiki/Ebenezer_Landells "Ebenezer Landells"), on an initial investment of £25 (equivalent to £2,146 in 2023). It was jointly edited by Mayhew and [Mark Lemon](/wiki/Mark_Lemon "Mark Lemon"). It was subtitled *The London Charivari* in homage to [Charles Philipon](/wiki/Charles_Philipon "Charles Philipon")'s French satirical humour magazine *[Le Charivari](/wiki/Le_Charivari "Le Charivari")*.[[1]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEAppelbaumKelly198114-1) Reflecting their satiric and humorous intent, the two editors took for their name and masthead the anarchic glove puppet Mr. Punch, of [Punch and Judy](/wiki/Punch_and_Judy "Punch and Judy"); the name also referred to a joke made early on about one of the magazine's first editors, Lemon, that "[punch](/wiki/Punch_(drink) "Punch (drink)") is nothing without lemon".[[2]](#cite_note-2)

Mayhew ceased to be joint editor in 1842 and became "suggestor in chief" until he severed his connection in 1845. The magazine initially struggled for readers, except for an 1842 *Almanack* issue which shocked its creators by selling 90,000 copies. In December 1842, due to financial difficulties, the magazine was sold to [Bradbury and Evans](/wiki/Bradbury_and_Evans "Bradbury and Evans"), both printers and publishers. Bradbury and Evans capitalised on newly evolving mass printing technologies and also were the publishers for [Charles Dickens](/wiki/Charles_Dickens "Charles Dickens") and [William Makepeace Thackeray](/wiki/William_Makepeace_Thackeray "William Makepeace Thackeray").

### Cartoon terminology

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Punch_(magazine)&action=edit&section=2 "Edit section: Cartoon terminology")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/f/f9/SubstanceandShadow.jpg/250px-SubstanceandShadow.jpg)](/wiki/File:SubstanceandShadow.jpg)

[John Leech](/wiki/John_Leech_(caricaturist) "John Leech (caricaturist)"), *Substance and Shadow* (1843), published as *Cartoon, No. 1*

The term "[cartoon](/wiki/Cartoon "Cartoon")" to refer to comic drawings was first used in *Punch* in 1843, when the [Houses of Parliament](/wiki/Palace_of_Westminster "Palace of Westminster") were to be decorated with murals, and "cartoons" for the mural were displayed for the public; the term "cartoon" then meant a finished preliminary sketch on a large piece of cardboard, or *cartone* in Italian. *Punch* humorously appropriated the term to refer to its political cartoons, and the popularity of the *Punch* cartoons led to the term's widespread use.[[3]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEAppelbaumKelly198115-3)

### Artistry

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Punch_(magazine)&action=edit&section=3 "Edit section: Artistry")]

Illustrator [Archibald Henning](/wiki/Archibald_Henning "Archibald Henning") designed the cover of the magazine's first issues. The cover design varied in the early years, though [Richard Doyle](/wiki/Richard_Doyle_(illustrator) "Richard Doyle (illustrator)") designed what became the magazine's [masthead](/wiki/Masthead_(British_publishing) "Masthead (British publishing)") in 1849. Artists who published in *Punch* during the 1840s and 1850s included [John Leech](/wiki/John_Leech_(caricaturist) "John Leech (caricaturist)"), Doyle, [John Tenniel](/wiki/John_Tenniel "John Tenniel"), and [Charles Keene](/wiki/Charles_Keene_(artist) "Charles Keene (artist)"). This group became known as "The *Punch* Brotherhood", which also included Charles Dickens, who joined Bradbury and Evans after leaving [Chapman and Hall](/wiki/Chapman_and_Hall "Chapman and Hall") in 1843.[[4]](#cite_note-vicweb-4) *Punch*'s authors and artists also contributed to another Bradbury and Evans literary magazine called *[Once A Week](/wiki/Once_A_Week_(magazine) "Once A Week (magazine)")* (est. 1859), created in response to Dickens' departure from *[Household Words](/wiki/Household_Words "Household Words")*.[[4]](#cite_note-vicweb-4)

[Helen Hoppner Coode](/wiki/Helen_Hoppner_Coode "Helen Hoppner Coode") contributed nineteen drawings to *Punch* and is recognised as its first woman contributor.[[5]](#cite_note-5)[[6]](#cite_note-6)

### Liberal competition

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Punch_(magazine)&action=edit&section=4 "Edit section: Liberal competition")]

In the 1860s and '70s, conservative *Punch* faced competition from upstart liberal journal *[Fun](/wiki/Fun_(magazine) "Fun (magazine)")*, but after about 1874, *Fun*'s fortunes faded. At Evans's café in London, the two journals had "round tables" in competition with each other.[[7]](#cite_note-7)

### Gaining a market and relations with other papers

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Punch_(magazine)&action=edit&section=5 "Edit section: Gaining a market and relations with other papers")]

After months of financial difficulty and lack of market success, *Punch* became a staple for British drawing rooms because of its sophisticated humour and absence of offensive material, especially when viewed against the satirical press of the time. *[The Times](/wiki/The_Times "The Times")* and the Sunday paper *[News of the World](/wiki/News_of_the_World "News of the World")* used small pieces from *Punch* as column fillers, giving the magazine free publicity and indirectly granting a degree of respectability, a privilege not enjoyed by any other comic publication. *Punch* shared a friendly relationship with not only *The Times*, but also journals aimed at intellectual audiences such as the *[Westminster Review](/wiki/Westminster_Review "Westminster Review")*, which published a 53-page illustrated article on *Punch*'s first two volumes. Historian [Richard Altick](/wiki/Richard_Altick "Richard Altick") writes that "To judge from the number of references to it in the private letters and memoirs of the 1840s...*Punch* had become a household word within a year or two of its founding, beginning in the middle class and soon reaching the pinnacle of society, [royalty](/wiki/Royal_family "Royal family") itself".[[8]](#cite_note-8)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/3/3d/True_humility.png)](/wiki/File:True_humility.png)

"True Humility": Bishop: "I'm afraid you've got a bad egg, Mr Jones"; Curate: "Oh, no, my Lord, I assure you! Parts of it are excellent!"  

[George du Maurier](/wiki/George_du_Maurier "George du Maurier"), 1895[[9]](#cite_note-9)

Increasing in readership and popularity throughout the remainder of the 1840s and '50s, *Punch* was the success story of a [threepenny](/wiki/Threepence_(British_coin) "Threepence (British coin)") weekly paper that had become one of the most talked-about and enjoyed periodicals. *Punch* enjoyed an audience including [Elizabeth Barrett](/wiki/Elizabeth_Barrett "Elizabeth Barrett"), [Robert Browning](/wiki/Robert_Browning "Robert Browning"), [Thomas Carlyle](/wiki/Thomas_Carlyle "Thomas Carlyle"), [Edward FitzGerald](/wiki/Edward_FitzGerald_(poet) "Edward FitzGerald (poet)"), [Charlotte Brontë](/wiki/Charlotte_Bront%C3%AB "Charlotte Brontë"), [Queen Victoria](/wiki/Queen_Victoria "Queen Victoria"), [Prince Albert](/wiki/Albert,_Prince_Consort "Albert, Prince Consort"), [Ralph Waldo Emerson](/wiki/Ralph_Waldo_Emerson "Ralph Waldo Emerson"), [Emily Dickinson](/wiki/Emily_Dickinson "Emily Dickinson"), [Herman Melville](/wiki/Herman_Melville "Herman Melville"), [Henry Wadsworth Longfellow](/wiki/Henry_Wadsworth_Longfellow "Henry Wadsworth Longfellow"), and [James Russell Lowell](/wiki/James_Russell_Lowell "James Russell Lowell"). *Punch* gave several phrases to the [English language](/wiki/English_language "English language"), including [The Crystal Palace](/wiki/The_Crystal_Palace "The Crystal Palace"), and the "[Curate's egg](/wiki/Curate%27s_egg "Curate's egg")" (first seen in an 1895 cartoon by [George du Maurier](/wiki/George_du_Maurier "George du Maurier")). Several British humour classics were first serialised in *Punch*, such as the *[Diary of a Nobody](/wiki/Diary_of_a_Nobody "Diary of a Nobody")* and *[1066 and All That](/wiki/1066_and_All_That "1066 and All That")*. Towards the end of the 19th century, the artistic roster included [Harry Furniss](/wiki/Harry_Furniss "Harry Furniss"), [Linley Sambourne](/wiki/Linley_Sambourne "Linley Sambourne"), [Francis Carruthers Gould](/wiki/Francis_Carruthers_Gould "Francis Carruthers Gould"), and [Phil May](/wiki/Phil_May_(caricaturist) "Phil May (caricaturist)").[[4]](#cite_note-vicweb-4) Among the outstanding cartoonists of the following century were [Bernard Partridge](/wiki/Bernard_Partridge "Bernard Partridge"), [H. M. Bateman](/wiki/H._M._Bateman "H. M. Bateman"), [Bernard Hollowood](/wiki/Bernard_Hollowood "Bernard Hollowood") (who also edited the magazine from 1957 to 1968), Kenneth Mahood, and [Norman Thelwell](/wiki/Norman_Thelwell "Norman Thelwell").

Circulation broke the 100,000 mark around 1910, and peaked in 1947–1948 at 175,000 to 184,000. Sales declined steadily thereafter; ultimately, the magazine was forced to close in 2002 after 161 years of publication.[[10]](#cite_note-mle-10)

*Punch* was widely emulated worldwide and was popular throughout the [British Empire](/wiki/British_Empire "British Empire"). The experience of Britons in British colonies, especially in India, influenced *Punch* and its iconography. Tenniel's *Punch* cartoons of the 1857 [Sepoy Mutiny](/wiki/Indian_Rebellion_of_1857 "Indian Rebellion of 1857") led to a surge in the magazine's popularity. India was frequently caricatured in *Punch* and was an important source of knowledge on [the subcontinent](/wiki/Indian_subcontinent "Indian subcontinent") for British readers.[[11]](#cite_note-11)

### Later years

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Punch_(magazine)&action=edit&section=6 "Edit section: Later years")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/8/81/Father_Christmas_Up-To-Date%2C_Punch%2C_Dec_1896.jpg/250px-Father_Christmas_Up-To-Date%2C_Punch%2C_Dec_1896.jpg)](/wiki/File:Father_Christmas_Up-To-Date,_Punch,_Dec_1896.jpg)

1896 cartoon by [John Tenniel](/wiki/John_Tenniel "John Tenniel") of [Father Christmas](/wiki/Father_Christmas "Father Christmas") driving an early car

*Punch* material was collected in book formats from the late 19th century, which included *Pick of the Punch* annuals with cartoons and text features, *Punch and the War* (a 1941 collection of WWII-related cartoons), and *A Big Bowl of Punch* – which was republished a number of times. Many *Punch* cartoonists of the late 20th century published collections of their own, partly based on *Punch* contributions.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

In early 1996, businessman [Mohamed Al-Fayed](/wiki/Mohamed_Al-Fayed "Mohamed Al-Fayed") relaunched *Punch*.[[10]](#cite_note-mle-10)[[12]](#cite_note-12) *Punch* never became profitable in its new incarnation, and at the end of May 2002, it was announced as once more ceasing publication.[[10]](#cite_note-mle-10) Press reports quoted a loss of £16 million over the six years of publication, with only 6,000 subscribers at the end.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

Whereas the earlier version of *Punch* prominently featured the clownish character [Punchinello](/wiki/Pulcinella "Pulcinella") (Punch of [Punch and Judy](/wiki/Punch_and_Judy "Punch and Judy")) performing antics on front covers, the resurrected *Punch* did not use the character, but featured on its weekly covers a photograph of a boxing glove, thus informing its readers that the new magazine intended its name to mean "punch" in the sense of a boxing blow.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

### *Punch* table

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Punch_(magazine)&action=edit&section=7 "Edit section: Punch table")]

In 2004, much of the archives was acquired by the [British Library](/wiki/British_Library "British Library"), including the *Punch* table. The long oval Victorian table was brought into the offices some time around 1855, and was used for staff meetings and on other occasions. The wooden surface is scarred with the carved initials of the magazine's long-term writers, artists, and editors, as well as six invited "strangers", including [James Thurber](/wiki/James_Thurber "James Thurber") and [Charles III](/wiki/Charles_III "Charles III") (then [Prince of Wales](/wiki/Prince_of_Wales "Prince of Wales")). [Mark Twain](/wiki/Mark_Twain "Mark Twain") declined the invitation, saying that the already-carved initials of William Makepeace Thackeray included his own.

## Influence

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Punch_(magazine)&action=edit&section=13 "Edit section: Influence")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/e/ee/Punch_in_Canada_-_1%286%29_-_1849-03-31_-_John_H._Walker.jpg/250px-Punch_in_Canada_-_1%286%29_-_1849-03-31_-_John_H._Walker.jpg)](/wiki/File:Punch_in_Canada_-_1(6)_-_1849-03-31_-_John_H._Walker.jpg)

A whole-page cartoon from *Punch in Canada*, volume 1 issue 6 (31 March, 1849), by its founder John H. Walker

*Punch* was influential throughout the [British Empire](/wiki/British_Empire "British Empire"), and in countries including Turkey, India, Japan, and China, with *Punch* imitators appearing in [Cairo](/wiki/Cairo "Cairo"), [Yokohama](/wiki/Yokohama "Yokohama"), [Tokyo](/wiki/Tokyo "Tokyo"), [Hong Kong](/wiki/Hong_Kong "Hong Kong"), and [Shanghai](/wiki/Shanghai "Shanghai").[[22]](#cite_note-22)

A Canadian version, *Punch in Canada*, was launched on 1 January 1849. The magazine was published by Thomas Blades de Walden, a dilapidated member of one of the great aristocratic families of England, and an associated of the officers of the garrison stationed in Toronto.[[23]](#cite_note-23) According to John Henry Walker, a wood engraver working for *Punch*, the magazine was doing well. However, the production ceased abruptly in 1850[[24]](#cite_note-24) when De Walden and Charles Dawson Shanly fled to New York.[[25]](#cite_note-25)

* *Punch* gave its name to the [Lucknow](/wiki/Lucknow "Lucknow")-based satirical [Urdu](/wiki/Urdu "Urdu") weekly *[Awadh Punch](/wiki/Awadh_Punch "Awadh Punch")* (1877–1936), which, in turn, inspired dozens of other "Punch" periodicals in India.[[26]](#cite_note-26)
* [University of Pennsylvania](/wiki/University_of_Pennsylvania "University of Pennsylvania") humour magazine the *[Pennsylvania Punch Bowl](/wiki/Pennsylvania_Punch_Bowl "Pennsylvania Punch Bowl")* derived its name from this magazine.
* Australia's *[Melbourne Punch](/wiki/Melbourne_Punch "Melbourne Punch")* was inspired by the London original.
* Charles Wirgman's *[Japan Punch](/wiki/Japan_Punch "Japan Punch")* (1862–1865, 1865–1887) was based on *Punch* and went on to inspire elements of modern *[manga](/wiki/Manga "Manga")*.[[27]](#cite_note-27)
* *China Punch*, established in 1867 in [Hong Kong](/wiki/British_Hong_Kong "British Hong Kong"), was the first humour magazine in greater China. It was followed in 1871 in treaty-port Shanghai by *Puck, or the Shanghai Charivari*.[[28]](#cite_note-28)[[29]](#cite_note-29)
* *Punch* along with founder Henry Mayhew were included in [Terry Pratchett](/wiki/Terry_Pratchett "Terry Pratchett")'s non-Discworld novel *[Dodger](/wiki/Dodger_(novel) "Dodger (novel)")*.
